1:
The chapter opens with a great muster, and the grammatical-historical commentators are busy first with a small word and a large number. Charles Ellicott would transpose the adverb — read it "David gathered together again" — and refers it to the former military musters, noting that 1 Chron. 13:1-4 records the consultations with the leaders of Israel that preceded this gathering, so that the thirty thousand were the more prominent representatives of "all Israel." Adam Clarke takes the figure differently, supposing a fresh levy by which David augmented his army by thirty thousand troops — and like the others he flags the rival reading, for the Septuagint has seventy thousand where the Hebrew, Targum, Syriac and Arabic all agree on thirty. Keil and Delitzsch lift the eye from the numbers to the design: having fixed on Zion as his capital, David turned to the worship of the congregation, "fallen grievously into decay since the death of Eli," and resolved to fetch the ark "from its obscurity" and make the royal city the centre of the nation's worship — the natural consequence of his standing as earthly monarch under Jehovah the God-king.

The reformed John Gill weighs the same word "again": the Jewish writers refer it to the gathering that made David king at Hebron, but he thinks it points rather to the recent war with the Philistines, the same choice young men now turned from battle to bring up the ark "with dancing and singing, and to protect it." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the muster for its pathos. The ark had lain at Kirjath-jearim, a neglected thing, ever since it came back from the Philistines (1 Sam. 7:1-2), "thrown aside" for many years; "perpetual visibility is no mark of the true church," for God is present with his people even when they lack the outward tokens — and now that David is settled the honour of the ark begins to revive (Phil. 4:10). He pictures the thirty thousand chosen men, with the common people besides (1 Chron. 13:5), gathered perhaps at one of the three great festivals, a noble cavalcade that would teach the young generation, who had "scarcely heard of the ark," to venerate it. Beneath the debate over the word and the number, the witnesses agree in what the day means: a treasure long buried is at last being sought, and a nation is learning again to think and speak highly of its God.
2:
The verse names the starting-place and heaps up a great title upon the ark, and the witnesses divide over a preposition and unite over a Name. The grammatical-historical readers wrestle with the text. Keil and Delitzsch note the awkward "from Baale of Judah" — the Hebrew has "from," which some rendered "from the rulers" or "citizens of Judah," but that is "decidedly incorrect," since the following word "thence" only makes sense if Baale-Jehudah is a place-name: another name for Kirjath-jearim, called Baalah in Josh. 15:9 and 1 Chron. 13:6. Charles Ellicott agrees the town is Kirjath-jearim, where the ark had rested since 1 Sam. 7:2, but suspects a textual error, reading "to Baale" rather than "from," the historian being so absorbed in his subject that he omits the journey out; he even weighs the site — the older identification at Kuryet-el-enab against the newer one at 'Erma, in either case some hours' march from the capital. Adam Clarke presses a nice point in the divine title: the word shem ("name") stands twice together, and one, he thinks, should be read sham, "there" — "There the name of the Lord of hosts was invoked."

On the title itself the commentators close ranks. Ellicott translates the clause more exactly — the ark "is called by the name, the name of Jehovah of hosts" — and reads it as the heart of the matter: the ark is "the visible symbol of God's presence and of His covenant with His people." Keil had made the same identification in the previous verse, that the ark is "the true centre of the Mosaic sanctuary," where the Lord "that dwelleth between the cherubim" was enthroned. The reformed John Gill notes only that David went "with all the people that were with him," the thirty thousand and as many more as chose to go. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws out the reason for so great a labour: the ark was that "by, or before which, they were to present their prayers to God for counsel and succour upon all occasions," and so David "put himself and his people to so great trouble and charge" precisely "because it was to fetch up the choicest treasure which they had." Whatever the quarrel over the letter of the verse, the witnesses agree in its worship: the object of all this trouble is the throne of the invisible God among his people.
3:
Here the narrative records the fatal misstep, and the witnesses are unanimous that the new cart was a sin, however well meant. The reformed John Gill states it flatly: "which was a great mistake, since it ought not to have been put upon a cart, old or new; it was to be borne upon men's shoulders, and carried by Levites only, and those of the family of Kohath," to whom no wagons had ever been given. He marvels that so many priests and Levites, and David "so well versed in the law of God," did not recall it, and supposes they were misled by the Philistines, who had carted the ark to Bethshemesh unpunished — forgetting that the Philistines were "an ignorant Heathen people" who could be winked at where Israel could not. He adds that the error was later seen and rectified, and refuses Spinoza's conjecture that the word "Kirjath-jearim" has dropped from the text. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley presses the same distinction: God "might wink at the Philistines, because they were ignorant of God's laws," yet be angry with Israel, "because they knew, or might have known the law of God"; but "their present transports of joy... and their greedy desire of having the ark of God removed, made them inconsiderate."

The grammatical-historical commentators concur and soften only the blame. Adam Clarke grants that the new cart, "which had never been applied to any profane or common purpose," was chosen "through respect," "but this was not sufficient, for the ark should have been carried on the shoulders of the priests; and the neglect of this ceremony was the cause of the death of Uzzah." Charles Ellicott traces the innocence of the mistake to time: the cart was a mark of respect (compare 1 Sam. 6:7), yet a violation of Num. 7:9, which required the Levites to bear the ark; "it is not necessary to suppose that David intended to violate the law," but the ark having lain neglected "for more than two generations, the exact requirements in regard to it may easily have passed out of mind." Keil and Delitzsch fill in the domestic scene — the ark had stood some seventy years in the house of Abinadab, and Uzzah and Ahio were his sons or grandsons — while Ellicott reads "in the hill" for "in Gibeah." The witnesses land together on a sober lesson: reverence is not measured by good intention but by obedience, and a new cart cannot excuse an old command forgotten.
4:
This verse is the quietest in the chapter, and the witnesses treat it largely as a problem of the text, for the Hebrew here plainly repeats itself. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott is blunt: "the text has undoubtedly suffered here through the repetition of a line by the scribes. The whole verse is omitted in the parallel passage in 1 Chron. 13, and the first half of it (which is a repetition of the previous verse) in the LXX." So what the English reads as a fresh statement — "they brought it out of the house of Abinadab which was at Gibeah, accompanying the ark of God: and Ahio went before the ark" — is, on his reckoning, a copyist's stumble, the same clause written twice. The one detail that survives the tangle is the picture of the procession itself: Ahio walking in front of the ark, and Uzzah, as the next verse will show, at its side.

Keil and Delitzsch, reading the verse together with what follows, turn to a second textual crux — the strange note that David and all Israel played "with all manner of instruments made of fir wood." The phrase "could only mean, with all kinds of instruments made of cypress wood; but this mode of expression would be a very strange one even if the reading were correct." The Chronicler has instead "with all their might and with songs," and Keil judges this "evidently the correct reading, from which our text has sprung," the Septuagint betraying its awareness of both by combining them. The reformed John Gill adds only a small puzzle of his own: whether the thing "brought out" was the cart, last mentioned, or, as some thought, the coffer holding the golden offerings the Philistines had sent back with the ark. Thin as the verse is, the witnesses make it serve a purpose. Where the sacred historians differ in a letter, these commentators do not hide the seam but examine it, confident that the substance stands: the ark of God was moving out of its long obscurity, attended by the sons of the house that had kept it, toward the city of David. The very labour they spend on so slight a verse shows the reverence in which they held the record, unwilling to pass over even a scribe's doubled line without an honest account of it.
5:
The procession breaks into music, and the witnesses divide over a single Hebrew phrase while agreeing on the spirit of the day. The grammatical-historical readers fasten on the odd note that the instruments were made "of fir wood." Adam Clarke is impatient with it: the place "should be corrected from the parallel place" in 1 Chron. 13:8, which reads "with all their might, and with singing"; the difference is one of a single Hebrew word — atsey ("woods") for oz ("strength") — and "this makes a good sense, the first makes none," the Septuagint agreeing with "might." Charles Ellicott is more cautious: the Chronicler's "with all their might and with songs" is generally thought the correct reading and differs only slightly in Hebrew, "yet the variation must have been ancient, since the LXX. combines the two." He glosses the rare word for the cornets as "some kind of metal instrument with bells or rings," which the Vulgate renders sistra, where Chronicles has "trumpets," and he reminds the reader that "played" here "means dancing accompanied by music" (compare 1 Sam. 18:7).

The reformed John Gill passes over the textual snarl to fix the direction of the joy: they "played before the Lord, that is, before the ark, which was a symbol of the presence of the Lord." That phrase — before the Lord — is where the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley lays the whole weight of the verse: "Public joy should always be as before the Lord, with an eye to him, and terminating in him. Otherwise it is no better than public madness, and the source of all manner of wickedness." It is a striking pastoral turn on a scene of harps and timbrels — that the sanctity of the mirth lies not in its loudness but in its object. The witnesses thus hold two things together without strain: a critic's honesty about a corrupted line, and a preacher's insistence that even the most exuberant worship is only worship when it is offered up to God and not merely enjoyed. The music of Israel is right precisely because it is played toward the throne of the cherubim, and the same day's joy, we will soon see, could not sanctify an act of disobedience beside it.
6:
At the threshing-floor the joy is cut short, and the witnesses gather around three questions: where it happened, why the oxen faltered, and what exactly Uzzah did wrong. On the place, the grammatical-historical readers admit ignorance. Keil and Delitzsch take "Nachon's threshingfloor" to mean "the threshing-floor of the stroke," while the Chronicler's "Chidon" means "the threshing-floor of destruction," the name given "not from its owner, but from the incident," and soon "supplanted by the name Perez-uzzah." Charles Ellicott doubts whether the word is a proper name at all, and offers a homely cause: "the turning aside of the oxen to snatch the scattered grain of the threshingfloor may have caused the trouble." The reformed John Gill reports the rabbinic tradition that Chidon was the very spot where Joshua stretched out his spear toward Ai, and guesses the floor lay near Jerusalem, perhaps Araunah's own.

On the offence the witnesses close ranks. Keil explains that Uzzah "stretched out his hand to lay hold of the ark, i.e., to keep it from falling over with the cart, because the oxen slipped" — a reflex of protection. Yet the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry insists the small deed was a real crime: "Uzzah was a Levite, but priests only might touch the ark," and the law was express — the Kohathites might carry it by the staves but "must not touch any holy thing, lest they die" (Num. 4:15). "Uzzah's long familiarity with the ark... might occasion his presumption, but would not excuse it," and Henry drives the maxim home: "Familiarity, even with that which is most awful, is apt to breed contempt." Adam Clarke agrees the ground was the plain prohibition of Num. 4:15-20, where the Levites were "forbidden to touch the ark on pain of death," and Uzzah "was the first that suffered for a breach of this law." The witnesses thus refuse to soften the scene into mere accident. The cause was earthly enough — stumbling oxen and a steadying hand — but the law it broke was not, and the very reflex that seemed to honour the ark betrayed a forgetfulness of the holiness for which David had gathered all Israel. What looked like rescue was, in the sight of the witnesses, an unbidden touch upon the throne of God.
7:
The stroke falls, and the witnesses face the hardest question in the chapter: why so terrible a punishment for so well-meant an act? None excuses the deed, but they answer the severity along two lines. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott concedes the penalty seems "exceeding severe" for "a well-meaning, though unlawful act," yet finds it "on reflection... a very necessary manifestation of the Divine displeasure," for it involved not only breaking the letter of the law but "a want of reverence for the majesty of God" and "a disposition to profane familiarity with sacred things." He quotes von Gerlach: Uzzah was "a type of all who, with good intentions... yet with unsanctified minds, interfere in the affairs of the kingdom of God, from the notion that they are in danger." The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reasons from God's wider purpose: though the transgression seems small, "it is reasonable God should make some present examples of his high displeasure against sins, seemingly small," for the "demonstration of his own exact and impartial holiness" and for "the greater terror and caution of mankind, who are very prone to have slight thoughts of sin"; there is thus "more of God's mercy, than of his justice, in such actions," the justice falling on one man but the benefit reaching all ages.

Keil and Delitzsch keep to the words, rendering the coming name Perez-uzzah as "a sudden tearing away from life," and note that the burning anger of the next verse is David's, not directed against God but against the calamity he traces to his own undertaking. On the eternal state of Uzzah the witnesses part company. Adam Clarke is warmly hopeful: Uzzah "sinned through ignorance and precipitancy; he had not time to reflect," and "no man can doubt of his eternal safety... doubtless the mercy of God was extended to his soul." Ellicott is careful to close no door but to open none either: such judgments "were, however, temporal, and give in themselves no indication of the treatment of the offender beyond the grave." Beneath the disagreement the witnesses are one in the lesson the death is meant to teach — that the holiness of God is not a thing to be handled with unwashed hands, and that a good motive can never sanctify a forbidden touch. The very mercy-seat, Henry will add, could not save the man who presumed upon it.
8:
The death leaves David not humbled but hot, and the witnesses divide over the temper of his anger. The verse says plainly that "David was displeased, because the Lord had made a breach upon Uzzah," and named the place Perez-uzzah, "the breach of Uzzah." The reformed John Gill reads the displeasure at its darkest: David "could not easily submit, and be reconciled to the providence; his heart was ready to rise up against God, and murmur at him for striking him dead in such an awful manner, for so small a matter as this might seem to be," the more so because the stroke halted the procession, "cast a damp" upon the day's joy, seemed an ill omen, and might be "improved by his enemies against him." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry is equally severe with David: the same Hebrew word marks God's anger and the king's, "as if God might not assert the honour of his ark... without asking David leave." "Shall mortal man pretend to be more just than God?" Henry asks; "David did not now act like himself, like a man after God's own heart" — and he lays down the rule, "when we lie under God's anger we must keep under our own."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott is gentler with the king. "More exactly," the word means David "was angry," and "the cause of his vexation was the Divine judgment upon Uzzah; yet it does not follow that he was angry with God, but rather was simply vexed and disturbed at this most untoward interruption of his plans." He notes that the same term for a "breach" is used of a sudden divine visitation in Ex. 19:22, and that the phrase "unto this day" is "extremely indefinite." The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley stands between: David was "displeased, or, grieved, both for the sin, and for God's heavy judgment; whereby their hopes were dashed, and their joys interrupted." So the witnesses draw a portrait in two shades. To some, this is a lapse in the man after God's own heart, a flare of resentment that would rather God had not vindicated his own holiness; to others, a natural grief and dismay at a joyful day broken open. All agree the breach was God's doing and just, and that the name David fixed to the ground would stand as a lasting warning where he had lately celebrated a breach upon his enemies.
9:
Anger turns to dread, and the witnesses weigh what kind of fear seized the king. The verse records that "David was afraid of the Lord that day, and said, How shall the ark of the Lord come to me?" Keil and Delitzsch read the shift plainly: "David's excitement at what had occurred was soon changed into fear of the Lord," so that he reasoned, "If merely touching the ark of God is punished in this way, how can I have it brought near me, up to the citadel of Zion?" — and therefore turned it aside. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the fear as wholesome and even needed: "the immediate effect of the judgment was to produce in David, and doubtless in all the people, that awe of the majesty of God in which they had shown themselves deficient," and "if this was at first excessive, it was soon moderated."

The reformed John Gill is sterner, faulting the flavour of the fear: David was afraid "lest he should be smitten for his error also," the more so "as he had discovered some resentment at the Lord's dealing with Uzzah, when he ought to have been still and quiet, and submitted to the will of God... confessed his own error, and been thankful for his sparing mercy." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws out both the error and the good in David's dread. There was amazement in it, "as if God sought advantages against all that were about him," so that it seemed safer to keep the ark "at a distance"; he should rather have said, "Let the ark come to me, and I will take warning by this to treat it with more reverence" — for, as the Lord says in Jer. 25:6, "Provoke me not, and I will do you no hurt." Yet Henry finds a right use in it too: David does not conclude that "Uzzah was a sinner above all men," but turns the judgment upon himself, conscious of his own unworthiness, praying with the Psalmist, "My flesh trembleth for fear of thee" (Ps. 119:120). This, Henry says, is what God intends in his judgments, "that others may hear and fear." So the witnesses hold the moment in tension: David's fear was in part a slavish shrinking that would push the holy thing away, and in part the beginning of that true awe the whole enterprise had lacked — the reverence that, rightly kept, would soon bring the ark home in safety.
10:
David sets the ark aside, and the witnesses turn from the frightened king to the fearless host who takes it in. On David's motive they agree with the verse: he "would not remove the ark of the Lord unto him into the city of David," and the reformed John Gill reads this as prudence rather than mere panic — David would "wait a little longer, until he had more thoroughly considered of it, and made himself acquainted with everything relative to the ark, that he might know how to behave for the future, without giving offence." Adam Clarke notes only that the house of Obed-edom "appears to have been very near the city," so David "lodged the ark with the nearest friend."

The grammatical-historical commentators are absorbed in identifying that friend. Keil and Delitzsch make Obed-edom a Levite of the Korahite line "whose duty it was... to watch over the ark," called "the Gittite" from his birthplace, the Levitical city of Gath-rimmon (Josh. 21:24). Charles Ellicott spreads the evidence out at length — Obed-edom "was a Levite," the name ("servant of Edom") "not uncommon," and the Obed-edoms of David's day were "porters of the Tabernacle, Levitical musicians," who took an active part in bringing the ark to Jerusalem; he even links him to the man of whom it is said, "God blessed him" (1 Chron. 26:4-5). But it is the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley who catches the heart of the verse. Obed-edom "knew what slaughter the ark had made among the Philistines and the Bethshemites. He saw Uzzah struck dead; yet invites it to his house, and opens his doors without fear, knowing it was a savour of death, only to them that treated it ill." He borrows Bishop Hall's cry: "O the courage of an honest and faithful heart! Nothing can make God otherwise than amiable to him: even his justice is lovely." Here the witnesses set two men side by side — a king who dares not take the ark, and a Levite who dares — and the contrast is not cowardice against bravado but a faith that has learned the ark is deadly only to the presumptuous and a blessing to those who welcome it aright.
11:
The three months at Obed-edom's house become, in the witnesses' reading, the great reversal of the chapter: the same ark that slew Uzzah now fills a household with blessing. The verse is spare — the ark "continued in the house of Obed-edom the Gittite three months: and the Lord blessed Obed-edom, and all his household" — and the reformed John Gill adds only that David and his men "returned to their habitations" for that season. But the commentators seize on the plain cause of the blessing. Adam Clarke asks and answers his own question: "The Lord blessed Obed-edom — And why? Because he had the ark of the Lord in his house. Whoever entertains God's messengers, or consecrates his house to the service of God, will infallibly receive God's blessing."

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley sets the reward directly against the judgment: "The same hand that punished Uzzah's presumption, rewarded Obed-edom's humble boldness. None ever had, or ever shall have reason to say, that it is in vain to serve God. Piety is the best friend to prosperity," and "his household too shared in the blessing. It is good living in a family that entertains the ark; for all about it will fare the better for it." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, gathering up the same thought, reports the old tradition from Josephus that Obed-edom, "before... poor, on a sudden, in these three months, his estate increased, to the envy of his neighbours," and turns it into counsel for households: "Let masters of families be encouraged to keep up religion in their families, and to serve God... with their houses and estates, for that is the way to bring a blessing upon all they have." So the witnesses read these quiet months as a deliberate lesson hung between the death of Uzzah and the triumph to come. The ark is not a capricious terror but a discriminating presence — "a savour of death unto death" to the presumptuous and "a savour of life unto life" to the welcoming — and the blessing on this one Levite's house is what at last emboldens the king to bring it home. Where reverence receives the holy God, the very thing that men feared becomes their gain, and none who serve him serve in vain.
12:
The report of Obed-edom's blessing draws the king out again, and the witnesses read both his motive and the reformed manner of his second attempt. On the motive, the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott guards David from any charge of envy: "the immediate reason for David's action was the knowledge of the blessings which had come to Obed-edom," yet "this implies neither jealousy nor a wish to deprive his subject of a blessing"; David had only "desisted in a fit of vexation and then of fear," and now "saw that such fear was groundless" and completed his unfinished purpose "with gladness," that is, "with festal shouts and rejoicings." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the blessing as a double encouragement: it showed "that God was reconciled to them, and his anger was turned away," and "that the ark was not such a burdensome stone as it was taken to be, but, on the contrary, happy was the man that had it near him." He turns at once to Christ, "a stone of stumbling, and a rock of offence, to those that are disobedient; but to those who believe he is a corner-stone, elect, precious" (1 Pet. 2:6-8), and draws the practical lesson that "the experience others have had of the gains of godliness should encourage us to be religious."

Keil and Delitzsch underscore that this time "David adhered strictly to the instructions of the law, as the more elaborate account given in the Chronicles clearly shows," the ark now "carried, and not driven, as before." The reformed John Gill supplies only that the news was brought by "some of his courtiers who had heard of it." Adam Clarke pauses over the ancient versions, noting that the Vulgate and Septuagint swell the verse with sevens of choirs, a calf and lambs, harmonious organs and a costly tunic — additions found "in no MS., nor in the Chaldee, the Syriac, nor the Arabic, nor in the parallel place" in 1 Chron. 15:25. So the witnesses present the turning-point of the chapter with one accord: the blessing on Obed-edom's house is the evidence that God is appeased and the ark no burden but a treasure, and David goes back for it not in presumption but in corrected obedience and festal joy, the stone that had proved deadly now welcomed as precious.
13:
The second procession begins with sacrifice, and the witnesses agree on the reform David has made while dividing over how often the offerings were repeated. First, all mark the corrected method. The reformed John Gill observes that "now David had seen the former mistake, and rectified it, and ordered the Levites to carry it, as they did upon their shoulders, with the staves therein." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott says the same: "David no longer presumed to violate the law, but took care that the ark should be borne by the proper persons," and points to the detailed account in 1 Chron. 15 of the sanctifying of priests and Levites for the task. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes the reform a maxim: "then we make a good use of the judgments of God on ourselves and others when we are awakened by them to reform and amend whatever has been amiss," and he reads the sacrifice at the outset as "atonement for their former errors and in a thankful acknowledgment" of the blessing on Obed-edom's house — "we are likely to speed in our enterprises when we begin with God and give diligence to make our peace with him."

The one real disagreement concerns the six paces. Keil and Delitzsch take the words to mean that "sacrifices of this kind were offered along the whole way, at the distance of six paces apart" — "a possible thing," though it need not imply a halt at every interval. Ellicott resists this: "as soon as the removal of the ark had been successfully begun, David offered sacrifices of thanksgiving and of prayer," and again when the journey was done (1 Chron. 16:1); "it is quite unnecessary to suppose that offerings were made at each six steps of the way, for although this might have been possible, it is not recorded. The work was begun and ended with solemn sacrifice." He adds that David offered these, like Solomon after him, "through the ministration of the priests." So the witnesses frame the renewed enterprise between two certainties and one open question: certain that the ark is now borne as the law required, certain that David opens the way with blood and praise, and content to differ over whether the altar smoked once at the start or every few steps along the road to Zion.
14:
David dances, and here the witnesses are most sharply divided — over the propriety of the dance and the meaning of the linen ephod. Most honour the act. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads it as thankfulness "expressed by his outward carriage, according to the manner of those times," and the ephod as the sign that "although he was king of Israel, yet he willingly owned himself to be the Lord's minister and servant," having "laid by his royal robes." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott agrees that the king, by joining a dance usually left to women (Ex. 15:20), "marked his strong sense of the importance of the occasion, and his readiness to do his utmost in God's honour." Keil and Delitzsch call dancing "an expression of holy enthusiasm... a customary thing from time immemorial," and take the white ephod as the priestly-charactered dress David wore "as the head of the priestly nation of Israel." The reformed John Gill pictures him leaping and skipping "as a lamb" for joy, notes the Targum's softening "he praised before the Lord with all his might," and observes that dancing before the ark "was not usual, and therefore was observed by Michal with contempt."

Against this chorus stands Adam Clarke, the chapter's lone skeptic of the dance itself. "Dancing is a religious ceremony among the Hindoos, and they consider it an act of devotion to their idols. It is evident that David considered it in the same light. What connection dancing can have with devotion I cannot tell." He will allow only that "unpremeditated and involuntary skipping may be the effect of sudden mental elation." On the ephod, a quieter debate runs: Ellicott is careful that it was "not, therefore, a peculiarly priestly dress," being worn by Samuel too (1 Sam. 2:18) and made of ordinary bad, not the high priest's shesh, "simply a garment worn by any one engaged in a religious service," whereas Wesley and Gill call it "the usual habit of the priests and Levites." Beneath these differences the witnesses converge on David's own confession, soon to be spoken to Michal: whatever the world made of it, the dance was "before the Lord," the whole-hearted self-forgetting of a king who reckoned no service too low for the honour of God.
15:
The whole nation brings the ark up "with shouting, and with the sound of the trumpet," and the witnesses read the verse for the breadth of the joy and its silent contrast with the past. The reformed John Gill fills out the crowd: not David only, but "the elders of Israel, and the captains over thousands," "besides the common people," so that "there might be as large a number with him now as before" — the multitude that had gathered for the first, failed procession is regathered for the triumph. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, reading the shout and trumpet, hears in them not mere noise but testimony: the people "expressing their own joy... and giving notice to all about them to rejoice with them," and he draws the civic lesson that "the public and free administration of ordinances, not only under the protection, but under the smiles, of the civil powers, is just matter of rejoicing to any people."

Keil and Delitzsch, carrying the sentence into what follows, catch the shadow that falls across the sunlit procession. As the ark came into the city, Michal "looked out of the window... and despised him in her heart"; and Keil marks the deliberate wording, that she is called "the daughter of Saul" rather than the wife of David, "because on this occasion she manifested her father's disposition rather than her husband's." He recalls what that disposition was: "In Saul's time people did not trouble themselves about the ark of the covenant" (1 Chron. 13:3); "public worship was neglected." So the witnesses set the national shout against the royal daughter's silent scorn. Here is a whole people, from elders and captains to the humblest, lifting one voice of trumpet-joy over the ark's homecoming — the very thing the house of Saul had let lie forgotten for a generation. The verse, in their reading, is the high tide of the chapter's gladness, and its last clause plants the seed of the quarrel that will close it: the gulf between a heart that shouts before the Lord and a heart that watches from a window and is ashamed. The public joy is genuine and free, and it is precisely such joy that offends the pride that once neglected the ark altogether.
16:
At the height of the triumph a cold eye watches from a window, and the witnesses read the verse as the collision of two houses. The narrative is stark: as the ark entered the city, "Michal Saul's daughter looked through a window, and saw king David leaping and dancing before the Lord; and she despised him in her heart." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott draws out the contrast that governs the whole scene: "the contrast is here strongly brought out between the spirit of Saul's house in which Michal had been brought up, and that of David. In Saul's time the ark had been neglected, and true religion was uncared for." Michal, he judges, "who had fallen in love with David as a brave hero, could not understand the religious enthusiasm which led him to rank himself among the common people before the Lord." It is not lewdness she truly resents but lowliness — a king who will lose his dignity in worship.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads her contempt through her father's eyes: she "despised" David "as one of a base and mean spirit, that knew not how to carry himself with that majesty which became his place." The reformed John Gill notes the plain fact that the ark was now borne "on the shoulders of the Levites," the reformed manner framing the very scene her scorn falls upon. Adam Clarke catches the inwardness of the sin and its concealment: "she did not blame him outwardly; she thought he had disgraced himself, but she kept her mind to herself." That hidden despising — "in her heart" — is where the witnesses lay the weight. The offence is not yet spoken; it festers within, a contempt for the exercises of religion that will break out in words when David comes home. The commentators find in the window a parable: piety looks foolish, even shameful, to those who have never shared it, and the daughter of a house that let the ark lie forgotten cannot see, in the dancing king, anything but a monarch making himself cheap. Two kingdoms meet in that glance — the one that shouted in the street and the one that sneered behind the glass — and the barrenness that ends the chapter is already forming in her heart.
17:
The ark comes to rest, and the witnesses attend to the tent that received it and the sacrifices that hallowed it. On the tent they are unanimous and careful to distinguish it from the older sanctuary. Charles Ellicott notes it was "not the tabernacle made for it in the wilderness, and which seems to have been now at Gibeon, but a special tent which David... had prepared for it." The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley agrees and reads David's mind in it: "Moses' tabernacle was still at Gibeon" (1 Chron. 16:39), "which David left there, because he designed to build a temple at Jerusalem with all speed." Keil and Delitzsch place the ark "in the Most Holy Place" of a tent "constructed according to the type of the Mosaic tabernacle," and read the burnt-offerings and peace-offerings as the rite "to consecrate the newly erected house of God."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lingers on the wisdom of the arrangement. David "would not bring it into a private house, no, not his own, lest it should seem to be too much engrossed, and people's resort to it... should be less free"; yet he "would not build a house for it, lest that should supersede the building of a more stately temple in due time," and so, "in imitation of Moses's tabernacle," lodged it under curtains. On the sacrifices Henry hears both praise and prayer: David offered them "in thankfulness to God that the business was now done without any more errors or breaches, and in supplication... for the continuance of his favour," and lays down that "all our joys must be sanctified both with praises and prayers." He supposes David penned the 132nd Psalm on this occasion, as Henry had earlier assigned the 68th to the setting out. The reformed John Gill adds only that the ark was brought into "the strong hold of Zion." So the witnesses present the ark's installation as a deliberately provisional glory: a tent, not a temple; a place open to all Israel, not shut in a palace; a lodging consecrated by sacrifice and hedged by a forward-looking restraint, for David will not let the good he can do now crowd out the better his son will build.
18:
The sacrifices ended, David turns to bless the people, and the witnesses debate in what capacity the king pronounced that blessing. On the fact they agree, comparing it with the great precedent. Keil and Delitzsch note that David "blessed the people in the name of the Lord, as Solomon did afterwards at the dedication of the temple" (1 Kings 8:55). The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads it as both prayer and prerogative: David "prayed to God to bless them, and particularly to reward them for the honour and respect they had now shown to his ark," for "the less is blessed of the better" (Heb. 7:7), and by it "let them know they had a king that loved them."

The disagreement is fine but real: did David here act the priest? Adam Clarke says he did — "David acted here as priest, for it was the general prerogative of the priests to bless the people; but it appears, by both David and Solomon, that it was the prerogative of the kings also." Charles Ellicott is careful to keep the offices apart: David blessed the people "as Solomon did at the dedication of the temple," and "in both cases this was eminently fitting; but such blessing is by no means to be mistaken for the peculiar priestly blessing for which the form was prescribed" in Num. 6:22-26. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley takes the middle road, that David blessed "as a prophet, and as their king, to whom by office it belongs, by all means, to seek his people's welfare" — the blessing being chiefly that "he heartily and solemnly prayed to God for his blessing upon them." Ellicott also parses the two kinds of offering behind the scene: the burnt-offerings "were dedicatory," the peace-offerings "eucharistic," meant "to supply the wants of the people by a religious feast of communion with God" (compare 1 Kings 8:62-65). So the witnesses show a king standing between altar and people, and quietly divide over the reach of his office — whether he borrowed the priest's function, exercised his own royal and prophetic right, or simply prayed as any father might for his household — while agreeing that the day's worship overflowed from sacrifice into benediction, and that a ruler who blesses his people in God's name shows a heart that loves them.
19:
David crowns the day with an open-handed feast, and the witnesses divide over what exactly he distributed while agreeing on the largeness of heart behind it. The verse gives "to every one" — men and women alike — "a cake of bread, and a good piece of flesh, and a flagon of wine." The grammatical-historical commentators handle the words with caution. Adam Clarke observes that "the words of flesh and of wine we add; they are not in the Hebrew," and that the Chaldee reads only "one part and one portion," though the other versions understand it as the English does. Charles Ellicott presses further: the phrase rendered "a good piece of flesh" is "a peculiar word, used only here and in" 1 Chron. 16:3, and the "flagon of wine" "should be translated a cluster of grapes or raisins." Keil and Delitzsch agree the middle term is disputed — many rabbis take it for "a piece of flesh or roast meat," but "the meaning... is uncertain, and has been much disputed" — while the cake was "a round cake of bread, baked for sacrificial meals."

The reformed John Gill passes over the philology, calling it simply a "dole" divided among the people. But it is the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry who reads the theology of the gift. He takes it for "a generous treat" rather than alms, a sharing in "the peace-offerings... that they might feast with him upon the sacrifice," and finds in it a principle: "when the heart is enlarged in cheerfulness the hand should be opened in liberality. As those to whom God is merciful ought to show mercy in forgiving, so those to whom God is bountiful ought to exercise bounty in giving." He recalls the feast of Purim, "observed with sending portions one to another" (Esth. 9:22), and notes shrewdly that David thereby "recommended himself to the people," for "every one is a friend to him that giveth gifts." So the witnesses, whatever they make of the disputed loaf and flagon, agree in the spirit of the scene: worship that ended in sacrifice and blessing now spills over into food for the whole nation, high and low, women as well as men. The king's joy in his God cannot be kept to himself; it opens his hand, and the people go home every one to his house carrying a token of a day of gladness before the Lord.
20:
David turns homeward to bless his own house and is met at the door by scorn, and the witnesses divide over whether Michal's charge held any truth or was pure slander. First they mark the tender irony of the setting. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry observes that David "returned to bless his household" — "to pray with them and for them, and to offer up his family thanksgiving" — and that even a minister with "prophets, and priests, and Levites about him... did not devolve the work upon them, but himself blessed his household." Yet "even the palaces of princes are not exempt from domestic troubles," for "David had pleased all the multitude of Israel, but Michal was not pleased." Charles Ellicott and John Gill note that she came out to meet him with "cutting irony," while Henry hears in her taunt — "How glorious was the king of Israel to-day!" — a contempt that "began in the heart, but out of the abundance of that the mouth spoke."

The true division concerns her accusation that David "uncovered himself in the eyes of the handmaids... as one of the vain fellows shamelessly uncovereth himself." Henry defends the king outright: "we have no reason to think that this was true in fact. David, no doubt, observed decorum, and governed his zeal with discretion," but "it is common for those that reproach religion... to put false colours upon it." The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads "uncovered" innocently, as done "by stripping himself of his royal robes, that he might put on a Levitical ephod." Adam Clarke, however, allows Michal some ground: "it is probable that David used some violent gesticulations, by means of which some parts of his body became uncovered," though "it is very probable that we cannot guess all that was implied in this reproach" — and he calls her "How glorious" "a strong irony." He notes too that David, in blessing his house, acted "according to the custom of the patriarchs, who were priests in their own families," citing that Stephen calls David a patriarch (Acts 2:29). Beneath the dispute the witnesses agree with Henry's diagnosis: what truly galled Michal was "his affection to the ark," and "the exercises of religion appear very mean in the eyes of those that have little or no religion themselves" — a woman with more of Saul's daughter in her than of David's wife.
21:
David answers the scorn, and the witnesses read his reply as a double stroke — a vindication grounded in God and a rebuke grounded in God's choice. The heart of it, all agree, is the phrase "It was before the Lord." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lays out the logic: "David first gives the true and sufficient reason of his conduct — what he had done was before the Lord, in honouring whom no man can be really humbled; and then he turns with a reproof to Michal." The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley paraphrases the retort: it was done "in his presence and service, which though contemptible to thee, is, and ever shall be honourable in mine eyes." Adam Clarke hears the sting in it, granting that "David felt the reproach, and was strongly irritated, and seems to have spoken to Michal with sufficient asperity."

The rebuke turns on the word "which chose me before thy father." Keil and Delitzsch note that "with the words 'who chose me before thy father and all his house,' David humbles the pride of the king's daughter" — the very throne from which she looks down on him was taken from Saul's line and given to David, "to appoint me prince over the people of the Lord." Ellicott sharpens the point: "God had set aside her father and his house for this very spirit of pride in which she was now indulging, and had chosen him instead." Wesley reads the same election as an obligation of love: God "took away the honour from him and his, and transferred it unto me, whereby he hath obliged me to love and serve him with all my might." The reformed John Gill adds that what David did "was designed for the honour and glory of God, and in thankfulness to him," so that "she ought not to have reproached him with it." So the witnesses find in David's answer no wounded vanity but a settled principle: the audience of worship is God, not the handmaids or the watching wife, and "in honouring whom no man can be really humbled." The very lowliness Michal despised is the fitting posture of a man who remembers that his crown was a gift, and who will therefore spend himself, however it looks, on the God who gave it.
22:
David presses his answer to a paradox, and the witnesses gather around its logic of glory-through-lowliness. The vow is defiant — "I will yet be more vile than thus, and will be base in mine own sight" — and the reformed John Gill reads it simply: "if this is to be vile, I will endeavour to be viler still; if to dance before the ark, and sing the praises of God, be reckoned a lessening of me, I will more and more be found in doing such things." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott catches a textual finesse in the second clause: "the LXX., not understanding this expression, has changed it to 'in thine eyes.' But the meaning is, that while Michal had charged him with making himself base in the eyes of the maidservants... he was ready to abase himself in his own eyes, to do anything, however humbling it might seem even to himself, which should be for the honour and glory of God." Adam Clarke expands the retort into a speech: "I am not ashamed of humbling myself before that God who rejected thy father because of his obstinacy and pride, and chose me in his stead... and even those maid-servants, when they come to know the motive of my conduct, shall acknowledge its propriety."

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley turns the verse into a rule for all who are mocked for godliness: "the more we are vilified for well doing, the more resolute therein we should be, binding our religion the closer to us, for the endeavours of Satan's agents to shame us out of it," and to be base means "I will always be ready to abase myself before God, and think nothing too mean to stoop to for his honour." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry completes the thought: David "will be base in my own sight, and will think nothing too mean to stoop to for the honour of God," for "the greatest of men is less than the least of the ordinances of Jesus Christ"; and those truly pious are at last "manifested in the consciences even of those that speak ill of them" (2 Cor. 5:11). So the witnesses read David's answer as the exact inversion of Michal's values. What she calls self-abasement he embraces as the road to honour — "of the maidservants... shall I be had in honour" — and the very lowering that shamed her becomes, in the reckoning of heaven and at last of men, his true exaltation.
23:
The chapter closes on a barren womb, and the witnesses divide over whether Michal's childlessness was a stroke of divine judgment or the natural end of a broken marriage. Those who read judgment are the majority. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry states it plainly: "she unjustly reproached David for his devotion, and therefore God justly put her under the perpetual reproach of barrenness," for "those that honour God he will honour; but those that despise him, and his servants and service, shall be lightly esteemed." The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley agrees: it was "because of her proud and petulant speech and carriage to David, which God justly punished with barrenness." The reformed John Gill adds that "she that vilified David brought a reproach upon herself, as barrenness was always reckoned," so that no descendant of hers "arrived to royal dignity," and cites Abarbanel's thought that Providence so ordered it "that the seed of David and of Saul might not be mixed."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott leans rather to human causes. Childlessness was "the severest privation to an Oriental woman," and he suggests that during Michal's "long separation from David, while he was an outlaw, and she was married to Phaltiel... they had become somewhat alienated," so that "when the totally different spirit by which they were animated was brought out on this occasion, David determined to have no further intercourse with her." Adam Clarke holds both possibilities together — "probably David never more took her to his bed; or God, in his providence, might have subjected her to barrenness" — but he turns the verse into a warning against Michal's very fault: she "formed her judgment without reason, and meddled with that which she did not understand," and "we should be careful how we attribute actions, the reasons of which we cannot comprehend, to motives which may appear to us unjustifiable," for "rash judgments are doubly pernicious; they hurt those who form them, and those of whom they are formed." So the witnesses end the chapter where it turned — on the difference between the heart that honours God and the heart that despises his worship. Whether her barrenness was a direct sentence from heaven or the quiet estrangement of a king who would no longer come near her, all agree it fell as the fitting close to a scorn that had reproached a dancing king for humbling himself before the Lord.
